summary: Garcia on fire, horn‑filled third set, festive New Year’s energy
blurb: The show bursts with high‑energy playing, highlighted by a horn‑laden third set featuring Tower of Power and Etta James on Turn On Your Lovelight. Jerry Garcia delivers scorching solos throughout, while Sugar Magnolia and Sugaree shine in the second set. The festive New Year’s vibe makes it a must‑listen for 1982 fans.
review:
On December 31, 1982 at the Oakland Auditorium Arena the Grateful Dead rang in the new year with an exuberant, horn‑rich marathon that still feels fresh today. The first set opened strong with “Cold Rain And Snow” and rolled through a tight run of classics, but it was the second set where the band really hit their stride – “Sugar Magnolia” segued into a blistering “Sugaree,” followed by an inspired “Playing In The Band → Drums → Space” that built to a joyous “Not Fade Away.”
The third set turned into a party with guest appearances from Tower of Power and Etta James, turning “Turn On Your Lovelight” into a soulful horn jam that many fans cite as the night’s highlight. Throughout the evening Jerry Garcia was “on fire,” his tone bright and his solos soaring, even if a few recordings bury his guitar slightly in the mix. Bob Weir contributed solid rhythm work, especially on “Lovelight,” while Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann kept the groove tight during the famed “playing‑drums‑fade away” sequence. The festive atmosphere and the guest‑filled finale make this New Year’s Eve show a standout entry in the early‑’80s catalog.
For listeners seeking the best audio experience, the FM audience recording (gd1982-12-31.FM.azjones.113090.flac16) offers a well‑balanced mix that captures both the horns and the band’s energy without sacrificing clarity – a solid choice even if it lacks the ultra‑crisp edge of the Miller soundboard tapes.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are clearly positive, praising Jerry’s fire and the horn‑laden third set; two others are mixed, noting a divisive third set but still highlighting strong moments. The overall tone leans positive, warranting a moderate +1.0 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Balances horns and band well, captures the high‑energy vibe of the night, and offers clear audio despite being an audience FM source.

key_highlights: Horn‑laden third set with Tower of Power and Etta James on Turn On Your Lovelight
Jerry Garcia’s “on fire” guitar work throughout, especially in the third set
Strong first‑set opener with Cold Rain And Snow and high‑energy Sugaree
Festive New Year’s Eve vibe that makes the show a 1982 fan favorite

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia is described as 'on fire' with great tone and scorching solos, though a few mixes bury his guitar slightly.
band_performance.Bob: Weir provides solid rhythm work; noted as okay on Lovelight but without standout praise.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drumming shines, especially during the 'playing‑drums‑fade away' sequence.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann locks in tight grooves throughout, complementing Hart in the dual‑drum sections.
